judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with concrete file/function citations and fixes; they are comparable, with Report 2 marginally crisper on the fail-closed prescriptions.

report_1.evidence: BadRequestError — the request itself is malformed (4xx). Retrying the same payload on the secondary will fail the same way. Inappropriate ... ContentPolicyError — the primary refused on content-policy grounds. Routing around a policy refusal to a second vendor is inappropriate ... AuthError — a credential/configuration failure. Silently falling over masks a broken deployment ... Inappropriate — this should surface loudly, not be swallowed. ... fail over only for genuinely transient classes and let the rest propagate

report_2.evidence: NON_RETRYABLE = (BadRequestError, ContentPolicyError, AuthError) ... replace the blanket except Exception with an explicit denylist of definitively non-retryable errors that re-raises instead of failing over, and fall over only on the rest

report_1.evidence: os.environ.get("STAGE") != "production" → return True. ... rate limiting is disabled outside production via a brittle exact-string match ... This is a fail-open footgun ... In production, redis.incr raises RedisError → return True. Assessment: fail-open on any Redis outage ... make the production check robust (e.g. explicit allow-list of non-prod stages, or default-to-enforce)

report_2.evidence: Path A — STAGE unset or not exactly "production" → returns True. ... a safety gate must fail closed everywhere ... Fix: invert the logic to fail closed by default ... Path D — production, redis.incr raises RedisError → returns True. Wrong — fails open. ... this must fail closed: except RedisError: return False.

report_1.evidence: input_text is the FULL intake document, which per the task and README contains sensitive personal information. Writing entire sensitive documents to logs is a serious data-protection violation ... metadata is logged wholesale ... drop input_text entirely ... replace the raw metadata dump with an explicit allow-list of known-safe, non-PII fields

report_2.evidence: record.input_text — the full intake document text verbatim. This is the sensitive PII itself, dumped into logs uncontrolled. record.metadata — the entire metadata blob, logged as-is with no allowlist. ... do not log input_text at all ... replace the raw metadata with {k: record.metadata[k] for k in AUDIT_METADATA_ALLOWLIST if k in record.metadata} against an explicit allowlist constant

report_1.evidence: read via os.environ.get("PII_SALT", "") — i.e. it defaults to the empty string. ... the output degrades to sha256(applicant_id) — an unsalted plain hash, with no error, warning, or log. ... require the salt — raise/refuse (fail closed) if PII_SALT is unset or empty rather than defaulting to ""

report_2.evidence: If PII_SALT is unset, os.environ.get("PII_SALT", "") defaults to the empty string, so the function silently hashes "" + applicant_id, i.e. an unsalted SHA-256 of the raw ID. ... treat a missing/empty PII_SALT as a hard error at startup or in the function, e.g.: ... raise RuntimeError("PII_SALT is required; refusing to pseudonymize without a salt")
